name: Newton District
short_description: BLM public land in Beaver County offering free rockhounding for agate, jasper, and quartz specimens. No permit required - bring a day pack and start collecting.
description:
The Newton District is a productive rockhounding area on Bureau of Land Management (BLM) public land in southwestern Utah's Beaver County. This region sits within Utah's Basin and Range geological province, where ancient volcanic activity and mineralization created excellent collecting opportunities for common quartz-family minerals. The area features exposed volcanic rock formations and scattered surface specimens that make it accessible to rockhounds of all experience levels.

This location is popular with both beginners and experienced collectors because the geology here supports abundant agate, jasper, and chalcedony deposits. The volcanic heritage of the region means you will find colorful banded specimens and translucent to opaque quartz varieties weathering out of the hillsides and washes. The terrain is relatively open with moderate vegetation, allowing easy hiking access to promising collecting zones.

Collecting at Newton District is straightforward: the land is open to public rockhounding under standard BLM personal use rules. No permits are required, and the 25-pound-per-day limit gives you plenty of room to gather quality specimens. The site works well for weekend trips from nearby Beaver or for longer regional rockhounding tours through central Utah.

Expect to spend 2-4 hours actively searching and collecting. The best strategy is to work the washes and exposed slopes where water erosion naturally concentrates lighter specimens and exposes fresh material. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and sturdy boots - the volcanic rock can be sharp and the exposure considerable in summer months.
history: The Newton District sits within the Sevier volcanic belt, where Oligocene to Miocene volcanic activity (roughly 30-20 million years ago) deposited thick layers of rhyolite, andesite, and tuff. Subsequent weathering and erosion over millions of years fractured these volcanic rocks and concentrated silica-rich minerals like agate and jasper in accessible surface deposits. The area has been known to local Utah rockhounders for decades as a reliable source of quality specimens.
tips: Search the drainage washes and gullies where water erosion concentrates lighter specimens - this is where the best material naturally collects. Look for the colorful banded patterns typical of Utah agates: reds, browns, tans, and whites are common. Use a small pry bar or hammer to break loose pieces from exposed volcanic rock faces, and check the soil around the base of outcrops where pieces weather free. Early morning collecting in fall and spring will give you cooler conditions and better light for spotting translucent material.
terrain_notes: The Newton District features rolling volcanic hills with moderate elevation around 5,400-5,800 feet. Terrain is mostly open with sparse sagebrush and rabbitbrush vegetation. Footing is uneven with scattered volcanic rock - sturdy boots strongly recommended. Most productive collecting areas are within 0.5 to 2 miles of parking pullouts. The landscape is semi-arid with occasional seasonal drainages.
best_season: April - June and September - October. Summer heat (June-August) can exceed 85-90 degrees F at this elevation. Winter snow and cold (November-March) limit access. Spring and fall offer ideal temperatures and often reveal fresh material after seasonal weather.
written_directions: From Beaver, take US 89 north approximately 8 miles. Look for BLM access points and informal pullouts on the east side of the highway in the vicinity of Newton Valley. The Newton District boundaries are not always clearly marked, so consult current BLM maps before heading out. Local rock shops in Beaver can provide more precise access information and recommend current productive areas.
road_conditions: US 89 is paved and well-maintained. Access roads to specific collecting areas may be unpaved but generally passable to passenger vehicles. Some pullouts require high-clearance vehicles - check conditions locally.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas along US 89 and BLM access roads. No developed facilities. Park off the road and be prepared for sandy or rocky surfaces. Capacity varies by location - most pullouts accommodate 2-5 vehicles.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Collect only loose material and small hand-sized specimens. Pack out all trash. Respect any posted closure areas.
rules:

nearest_services: Beaver, approximately 8 miles south. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain with uneven, rocky footing. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Sturdy hiking boots required due to sharp volcanic rock. Most collecting sites involve short walks (under 2 miles) from parking areas. Open terrain with minimal obstacles makes navigation straightforward.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can I find at Newton District in Utah?
faq.answer: The Newton District is known for banded Utah agates in reds, browns, tans, and creams, plus reddish and multicolored jasper specimens. You will also find plain and banded quartz and chalcedony. Most specimens are small to medium sized and appear as loose pieces or partially exposed in volcanic rock outcrops.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at Newton District, and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding is completely free on this BLM public land. No permit is required for personal use collection. You are limited to 25 lbs per day under federal regulations, but this allows plenty of quality specimens for a day trip.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Newton District near Beaver, Utah?
faq.answer: Spring (April-June) and fall (September-October) are ideal. Summer heat exceeds 85-90 degrees F, and winter conditions can bring snow and cold. Spring and fall also often reveal fresh material exposed by seasonal weather.

faq.question: Can I bring my family and kids rockhounding at Newton District?
faq.answer: Absolutely. The Newton District is beginner and family-friendly with easy access and open terrain. Children ages 6 and up can enjoy searching for colorful agate and jasper specimens. The area is safe and does not require special skills or equipment.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Newton District?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots, sun protection, and plenty of water. A small pry bar or rock hammer helps loosen specimens from outcrops. A backpack or collection bag, field guides, and a headlamp or flashlight are useful. The volcanic rock is sharp, so wear durable clothing and gloves if desired.

faq.question: How far is Newton District from Beaver, Utah, and what are directions?
faq.answer: Newton District is about 8 miles north of Beaver. Take US 89 north from town and look for BLM pullouts and parking areas near Newton Valley on the east side of the highway. Local rock shops in Beaver can provide specific access details and recommend current collecting hotspots.
